1→# Intermediate Full Stack Software Engineer — Interview Preparation Kit
     2→
     3→| | |
     4→|---|---|
     5→| **Company:** | LekkeSlaap (owned by Tripco) |
     6→| **Website:** | https://www.lekkeslaap.co.za |
     7→| **Location:** | V&A; Waterfront, Cape Town (Hybrid) |
     8→| **Role Type:** | Full-time, Intermediate Full Stack |
     9→| **Candidate:** | Byron Devin |
    10→| **Prepared:** | March 2026 |
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Contents
    15→
    16→1. [Company Overview & Product Landscape](#1-company-overview--product-landscape)
    17→2. [Role Deep-Dive & What They're Evaluating](#2-role-deep-dive--what-theyre-evaluating)
    18→3. [Tech Stack Alignment & Gap Analysis](#3-tech-stack-alignment--gap-analysis)
    19→4. [Your Killer Stories (STAR Framework)](#4-your-killer-stories-star-framework)
    20→5. [Core Interview Questions & Rehearsed Answers](#5-core-interview-questions--rehearsed-answers)
    21→6. [Technical Deep-Dive Prep](#6-technical-deep-dive-prep)
    22→7. [Behavioural & Culture-Fit Questions](#7-behavioural--culture-fit-questions)
    23→8. [Questions to Ask Them](#8-questions-to-ask-them)
    24→9. [Loom Video Strategy (Afrikaans Opportunity)](#9-loom-video-strategy-afrikaans-opportunity)
    25→10. [Interview Delivery Tips](#10-interview-delivery-tips)
    26→
    27→---
    28→
    29→## 1. Company Overview & Product Landscape
    30→
    31→LekkeSlaap is South Africa's leading accommodation booking platform, launched in 2013 by Tripco, a travel technology company headquartered at the V&A; Waterfront in Cape Town. The platform lists over 38,000 properties including holiday homes, B&Bs;, guesthouses, hotels, and lodges across South Africa.
    32→
    33→Tripco also owns TravelGround.com, Viya.co.za, and LekkeVlieg.co.za (flights). They power white-label travel booking for brands like Sanlam Reality, Edgars Club, and First Car Hire. The company has 150+ employees and has won South Africa's Leading Online Travel Agency at the World Travel Awards for three consecutive years.
    34→
    35→### Key Product Features
    36→
    37→- Mobile-first booking experience with native iOS and Android apps
    38→- Bilingual platform (Afrikaans & English) — unique market positioning
    39→- LekkeSlaap Rewards tiered loyalty programme
    40→- Dedicated travel agent for every guest, available daily until midnight
    41→- Property management tools for hosts and accommodation owners
    42→- Automated payout system (Stitch Payouts integration) for property owners
    43→
    44→### Why This Matters for You
    45→
    46→LekkeSlaap is a high-traffic, consumer-facing product — exactly the environment the job spec emphasises. Your experience building user-facing products at Mohara (EA Inclusion, Agricarbon, Rock My Wedding) maps directly. Your Afrikaans fluency is a significant differentiator given the brand's bilingual identity.
    47→
    48→---
    49→
    50→## 2. Role Deep-Dive & What They're Evaluating
    51→
    52→### What They Want
    53→
    54→- 3–5 years full-stack experience in user-facing, high-traffic products
    55→- Proficiency across their stack: PHP (Laravel), React, Twig, MySQL/MariaDB, Redis, Nginx
    56→- Active use of AI tools and automation in development workflow
    57→- Cross-functional collaboration with Product, Design, and Engineering
    58→- Feature ownership end-to-end: from idea through to production and maintenance
    59→- Performance optimisation, secure coding, and clean documentation
    60→
    61→### What They're Really Evaluating
    62→
    63→- **Production maturity:** Can you own features on a platform serving millions of users?
    64→- **Stack adaptability:** You don't use PHP/Laravel daily, but can you ramp up fast? (Your Django self-teaching story proves this.)
    65→- **AI-forward mindset:** This is mentioned 6+ times in the job spec — it's clearly a strategic priority. You shaped AI standards at Mohara.
    66→- **Ownership & urgency:** They want doers. Your 7-bugs-in-5-days story and solo tech lead role demonstrate this perfectly.
    67→- **Cultural fit:** Small, close-knit team at the Waterfront. They want collaborative, adaptable people who take initiative.
    68→
    69→---
    70→
    71→## 3. Tech Stack Alignment & Gap Analysis
    72→
    73→| Technology | Their Stack | Your Experience | Gap? |
    74→|---|---|---|---|
    75→| Backend | PHP (Laravel) | Django, Node.js, Express, tRPC | Learn Laravel basics |
    76→| Frontend | React, Twig, HTML/CSS/JS | React, Next.js, TypeScript, Tailwind | Strong match |
    77→| Database | MySQL (MariaDB), Redis | PostgreSQL, MySQL | Strong match |
    78→| Infra | Nomad, Consul, Vault, Terraform | Vercel, AWS | Study HashiCorp |
    79→| CI/CD | Docker, GitHub Actions | Docker, CI/CD experience | Good match |
    80→| AI/Automation | AI-driven tools, automation | AI standards lead at Mohara | Strong match |
    81→| Testing | Automated testing | Jest, Vitest, Playwright, Django TDD | Strong match |
    82→| Languages | Python, C++, Rust (microservices) | Python, TypeScript | Aware of C++/Rust |
    83→
    84→### How to Address the PHP/Laravel Gap
    85→
    86→This is your biggest gap on paper, but it's very manageable. Frame it using your proven track record of rapid self-teaching:
    87→
    88→- You taught yourself Python/Django on the job at Agricarbon while maintaining delivery — same pattern applies to Laravel.
    89→- Laravel and Django share many concepts: MVC architecture, ORM, migrations, routing, middleware, Blade/Twig templating vs Django templates.
    90→- Spend 2–3 hours on Laravel basics before the interview: routing, Eloquent ORM, Blade vs Twig, artisan CLI. Enough to speak intelligently.
    91→- Emphasise your framework-agnostic thinking and fast learning velocity.
    92→
    93→### HashiCorp Stack Primer (Talking Points)
    94→
    95→- **Nomad:** Workload orchestrator (like K8s but simpler). Deploys containers, binaries, and Java apps.
    96→- **Consul:** Service discovery and health checking. Services register themselves; others find them via DNS/API.
    97→- **Vault:** Secrets management. Dynamic credentials, encryption as a service, access policies.
    98→- **Terraform:** Infrastructure as code. Declarative config files to provision and manage infra.
    99→- Together they form the 'HashiStack' — a self-managed alternative to K8s + cloud-native services.
   100→
   101→---
   102→
   103→## 4. Your Killer Stories (STAR Framework)
   104→
   105→Prepare these stories in advance. Each maps directly to what LekkeSlaap is evaluating. Use the STAR format: Situation, Task, Action, Result.
   106→
   107→### Performance Under Pressure (maps to: Performance & Security, Problem Resolution)
   108→
   109→EA Inclusion dashboard was loading in 48 seconds under scaling pressure. As sole tech lead, I diagnosed the root cause as unoptimised database queries and missing indexes. Refactored the query architecture and implemented strategic indexing, cutting load time to 3.6s — a 92% improvement. This directly unblocked client usage at scale.
   110→
   111→### Urgency & Ownership (maps to: Urgency & ownership, Problem Resolution)
   112→
   113→Before a major client release at EA Inclusion, 7 critical scoring bugs were discovered. I owned the triage, fixed all 7 in 5 days, achieving 100% scoring accuracy. This required deep debugging, clear prioritisation, and direct client communication throughout.
   114→
   115→### Rapid Stack Adoption (maps to: Adaptability, Continuous Learning)
   116→
   117→Joined the Agricarbon project which used Django/Python — technologies I hadn't used professionally. Self-taught Python and Django while maintaining delivery on a fixed-term contract. Within weeks I was contributing full-stack features and eventually led the frontend architecture.
   118→
   119→### AI-Forward Engineering (maps to: Automation & AI Enablement)
   120→
   121→At Mohara, I championed AI-augmented development practices across the company. Contributed to company-wide AI standards, led knowledge-sharing sessions, and actively integrated AI tools into my daily workflow for code generation, debugging, testing, and documentation.
   122→
   123→### Cross-Functional Ownership (maps to: Stakeholder Engagement, Product Feature Ownership)
   124→
   125→As solo tech lead on EA Inclusion, I own the full client relationship: running demos, managing prioritisation, shaping technical strategy, and translating business needs into technical solutions. Introduced feature flags and structured RCA processes to improve release confidence.
   126→
   127→### Mentorship & Team Growth (maps to: Collaboration, Code Quality)
   128→
   129→At Agricarbon, I mentored junior developers through 1:1 sessions and restructured epics with story points, reducing ticket count by 30–50%. This improved team velocity and code quality while fostering a collaborative learning environment.
   130→
   131→---
   132→
   133→## 5. Core Interview Questions & Rehearsed Answers
   134→
   135→These are the most likely questions based on the job spec. Rehearse each answer to be 60–120 seconds when spoken aloud.
   136→
   137→### Q: Tell us about yourself.
   138→
   139→I'm Byron, a fullstack software engineer based in Cape Town with 3+ years of professional experience. I started at Mohara as an associate and progressed to tech lead, where I now own an enterprise product end-to-end — from architecture and client demos to production deployments. My core stack is TypeScript, React, Next.js, and Python/Django, and I've built a strong track record of performance optimisation, rapid bug resolution, and AI-augmented development. I'm also fluent in Afrikaans, which I know is close to LekkeSlaap's heart. I'm excited about this role because it combines high-traffic consumer products, a modern tech stack, and a team that values ownership and innovation.
   140→
   141→### Q: Why LekkeSlaap / Tripco?
   142→
   143→Three reasons. First, LekkeSlaap is a product I genuinely admire — it's a homegrown South African platform that competes with international players through superior local knowledge and user experience. Second, the engineering culture resonates with me: a close-knit team, cross-functional collaboration, and a clear emphasis on AI and automation as strategic priorities — that's exactly how I like to work. Third, I'm based in Cape Town and the V&A; Waterfront hybrid model is ideal. I want to contribute to a product that millions of South Africans use.
   144→
   145→### Q: Your stack is TypeScript/React/Django. How would you handle our PHP/Laravel backend?
   146→
   147→I actually see this as a strength, not a gap. At Agricarbon, I joined a Django/Python project having never used either professionally — I taught myself on the job while maintaining delivery on a fixed deadline. Laravel and Django share architectural DNA: MVC patterns, ORMs, migrations, middleware, and templating engines. I'm already comfortable with MySQL from my Agricarbon work. My approach would be the same: immerse myself, pair with experienced teammates, and get productive fast. I'm not married to any framework — I'm focused on solving problems well.
   148→
   149→### Q: How do you use AI in your development workflow?
   150→
   151→AI is deeply embedded in how I work. At Mohara, I didn't just use AI tools — I helped shape the company's AI standards and led knowledge-sharing sessions. Day-to-day, I use AI for code generation, debugging assistance, writing tests, improving documentation, and exploring architectural approaches. But I'm thoughtful about it: I review AI output critically, I understand the limitations, and I focus on using it to amplify my productivity rather than replace understanding. I'd be excited to help LekkeSlaap expand AI-driven automation in testing, deployments, and product features.
   152→
   153→### Q: Describe a time you optimised performance on a production system.
   154→
   155→On EA Inclusion, our main dashboard was loading in 48 seconds — a crisis because the client was scaling usage. I profiled the system and found the root cause was unoptimised database queries hitting PostgreSQL without proper indexing. I refactored the query architecture, added strategic indexes, and restructured how data was fetched. Load time dropped to 3.6 seconds — a 92% improvement. The key lesson was that performance is often about data access patterns, not just frontend optimisation, which I know is critical for a high-traffic platform like LekkeSlaap.
   156→
   157→### Q: How do you approach owning a feature end-to-end?
   158→
   159→I live this daily as the solo tech lead on EA Inclusion. My process is: understand the business need through stakeholder conversations, break it into technical tasks with clear acceptance criteria, implement across the full stack, write tests, deploy with feature flags for safe rollout, and then own the monitoring and iteration. I also run client demos and handle feedback loops. The feature flag system and structured RCA process I introduced have significantly reduced regression risk and improved release confidence.
   160→
   161→---
   162→
   163→## 6. Technical Deep-Dive Prep
   164→
   165→Expect technical questions about architecture, performance, security, and their specific stack. Here are the key areas to prepare.
   166→
   167→### Full-Stack Architecture (High-Traffic Travel Platform)
   168→
   169→- **Caching strategies:** Redis for session management, page caching, search result caching. Discuss cache invalidation approaches.
   170→- **Database optimisation:** Query optimisation, indexing strategies for MySQL/MariaDB, read replicas for scaling reads.
   171→- **Search performance:** How would you build a fast accommodation search with filters (location, dates, price, amenities)?
   172→- **API design:** RESTful patterns, pagination, rate limiting, versioning. How to handle booking transactions atomically.
   173→- **Frontend performance:** Code splitting, lazy loading, image optimisation (critical for accommodation photos), SSR vs CSR trade-offs.
   174→
   175→### Security (Booking Platform Context)
   176→
   177→- PCI compliance considerations for payment processing
   178→- CSRF protection in Laravel (built-in middleware) and React forms
   179→- Input sanitisation and SQL injection prevention via ORM
   180→- Rate limiting on booking and authentication endpoints
   181→- POPIA compliance for South African user data
   182→
   183→### Infrastructure & DevOps Talking Points
   184→
   185→- Docker containerisation: multi-stage builds, docker-compose for local dev, production images
   186→- GitHub Actions: CI/CD pipeline structure — lint, test, build, deploy stages
   187→- HashiCorp Nomad vs Kubernetes: Nomad is simpler, single binary, supports non-container workloads
   188→- Terraform: declarative infrastructure, state management, plan/apply workflow
   189→- Monitoring and observability: logging, metrics, alerting for a booking platform
   190→
   191→### Quick Laravel Concepts to Know
   192→
   193→- **Eloquent ORM:** Active Record pattern (vs Django's Data Mapper). Models map directly to tables.
   194→- **Blade Templating:** Laravel's template engine. Similar concept to Django templates. Twig is also used at Tripco.
   195→- **Artisan CLI:** Like Django's manage.py. Migrations, seeding, custom commands.
   196→- **Middleware:** Same concept as Django middleware. Authentication, CORS, CSRF handled here.
   197→- **Laravel Mix / Vite:** Asset bundling. Compiles JS/CSS for production.

I follow industry trends, experiment with new tools, and actively bring innovations back to the team. My progression from associate to tech lead in ~2 years reflects this growth mindset.

   226→---

# Intermediate Full Stack Software Engineer — Interview Preparation Kit

| | |
|---|---|
| **Company:** | LekkeSlaap (owned by Tripco) |
| **Website:** | https://www.lekkeslaap.co.za |
| **Location:** | V&A; Waterfront, Cape Town (Hybrid) |
| **Role Type:** | Full-time, Intermediate Full Stack |
| **Candidate:** | Byron Devin |
| **Prepared:** | March 2026 |

---

## Contents

1. [Company Overview & Product Landscape](#1-company-overview--product-landscape)
2. [Role Deep-Dive & What They're Evaluating](#2-role-deep-dive--what-theyre-evaluating)
3. [Tech Stack Alignment & Gap Analysis](#3-tech-stack-alignment--gap-analysis)
4. [Your Killer Stories (STAR Framework)](#4-your-killer-stories-star-framework)
5. [Core Interview Questions & Rehearsed Answers](#5-core-interview-questions--rehearsed-answers)
6. [Technical Deep-Dive Prep](#6-technical-deep-dive-prep)
7. [Behavioural & Culture-Fit Questions](#7-behavioural--culture-fit-questions)
8. [Questions to Ask Them](#8-questions-to-ask-them)
9. [Loom Video Strategy (Afrikaans Opportunity)](#9-loom-video-strategy-afrikaans-opportunity)
10. [Interview Delivery Tips](#10-interview-delivery-tips)

---

## 1. Company Overview & Product Landscape

LekkeSlaap is South Africa's leading accommodation booking platform, launched in 2013 by Tripco, a travel technology company headquartered at the V&A; Waterfront in Cape Town. The platform lists over 38,000 properties including holiday homes, B&Bs;, guesthouses, hotels, and lodges across South Africa.

Tripco also owns TravelGround.com, Viya.co.za, and LekkeVlieg.co.za (flights). They power white-label travel booking for brands like Sanlam Reality, Edgars Club, and First Car Hire. The company has 150+ employees and has won South Africa's Leading Online Travel Agency at the World Travel Awards for three consecutive years.

### Key Product Features

- Mobile-first booking experience with native iOS and Android apps
- Bilingual platform (Afrikaans & English) — unique market positioning
- LekkeSlaap Rewards tiered loyalty programme
- Dedicated travel agent for every guest, available daily until midnight
- Property management tools for hosts and accommodation owners
- Automated payout system (Stitch Payouts integration) for property owners

### Why This Matters for You

LekkeSlaap is a high-traffic, consumer-facing product — exactly the environment the job spec emphasises. Your experience building user-facing products at Mohara (EA Inclusion, Agricarbon, Rock My Wedding) maps directly. Your Afrikaans fluency is a significant differentiator given the brand's bilingual identity.

---

## 2. Role Deep-Dive & What They're Evaluating

### What They Want

- 3–5 years full-stack experience in user-facing, high-traffic products
- Proficiency across their stack: PHP (Laravel), React, Twig, MySQL/MariaDB, Redis, Nginx
- Active use of AI tools and automation in development workflow
- Cross-functional collaboration with Product, Design, and Engineering
- Feature ownership end-to-end: from idea through to production and maintenance
- Performance optimisation, secure coding, and clean documentation

### What They're Really Evaluating

- **Production maturity:** Can you own features on a platform serving millions of users?
- **Stack adaptability:** You don't use PHP/Laravel daily, but can you ramp up fast? (Your Django self-teaching story proves this.)
- **AI-forward mindset:** This is mentioned 6+ times in the job spec — it's clearly a strategic priority. You shaped AI standards at Mohara.
- **Ownership & urgency:** They want doers. Your 7-bugs-in-5-days story and solo tech lead role demonstrate this perfectly.
- **Cultural fit:** Small, close-knit team at the Waterfront. They want collaborative, adaptable people who take initiative.

---

## 3. Tech Stack Alignment & Gap Analysis

| Technology | Their Stack | Your Experience | Gap? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Backend | PHP (Laravel) | Django, Node.js, Express, tRPC | Learn Laravel basics |
| Frontend | React, Twig, HTML/CSS/JS | React, Next.js, TypeScript, Tailwind | Strong match |
| Database | MySQL (MariaDB), Redis | PostgreSQL, MySQL | Strong match |
| Infra | Nomad, Consul, Vault, Terraform | Vercel, AWS | Study HashiCorp |
| CI/CD | Docker, GitHub Actions | Docker, CI/CD experience | Good match |
| AI/Automation | AI-driven tools, automation | AI standards lead at Mohara | Strong match |
| Testing | Automated testing | Jest, Vitest, Playwright, Django TDD | Strong match |
| Languages | Python, C++, Rust (microservices) | Python, TypeScript | Aware of C++/Rust |

### How to Address the PHP/Laravel Gap

This is your biggest gap on paper, but it's very manageable. Frame it using your proven track record of rapid self-teaching:

- You taught yourself Python/Django on the job at Agricarbon while maintaining delivery — same pattern applies to Laravel.
- Laravel and Django share many concepts: MVC architecture, ORM, migrations, routing, middleware, Blade/Twig templating vs Django templates.
- Spend 2–3 hours on Laravel basics before the interview: routing, Eloquent ORM, Blade vs Twig, artisan CLI. Enough to speak intelligently.
- Emphasise your framework-agnostic thinking and fast learning velocity.

### HashiCorp Stack Primer (Talking Points)

- **Nomad:** Workload orchestrator (like K8s but simpler). Deploys containers, binaries, and Java apps.
- **Consul:** Service discovery and health checking. Services register themselves; others find them via DNS/API.
- **Vault:** Secrets management. Dynamic credentials, encryption as a service, access policies.
- **Terraform:** Infrastructure as code. Declarative config files to provision and manage infra.
- Together they form the 'HashiStack' — a self-managed alternative to K8s + cloud-native services.

---

## 4. Your Killer Stories (STAR Framework)

Prepare these stories in advance. Each maps directly to what LekkeSlaap is evaluating. Use the STAR format: Situation, Task, Action, Result.

### Performance Under Pressure (maps to: Performance & Security, Problem Resolution)

EA Inclusion dashboard was loading in 48 seconds under scaling pressure. As sole tech lead, I diagnosed the root cause as unoptimised database queries and missing indexes. Refactored the query architecture and implemented strategic indexing, cutting load time to 3.6s — a 92% improvement. This directly unblocked client usage at scale.

### Urgency & Ownership (maps to: Urgency & ownership, Problem Resolution)

Before a major client release at EA Inclusion, 7 critical scoring bugs were discovered. I owned the triage, fixed all 7 in 5 days, achieving 100% scoring accuracy. This required deep debugging, clear prioritisation, and direct client communication throughout.

### Rapid Stack Adoption (maps to: Adaptability, Continuous Learning)

Joined the Agricarbon project which used Django/Python — technologies I hadn't used professionally. Self-taught Python and Django while maintaining delivery on a fixed-term contract. Within weeks I was contributing full-stack features and eventually led the frontend architecture.

### AI-Forward Engineering (maps to: Automation & AI Enablement)

At Mohara, I championed AI-augmented development practices across the company. Contributed to company-wide AI standards, led knowledge-sharing sessions, and actively integrated AI tools into my daily workflow for code generation, debugging, testing, and documentation.

### Cross-Functional Ownership (maps to: Stakeholder Engagement, Product Feature Ownership)

As solo tech lead on EA Inclusion, I own the full client relationship: running demos, managing prioritisation, shaping technical strategy, and translating business needs into technical solutions. Introduced feature flags and structured RCA processes to improve release confidence.

### Mentorship & Team Growth (maps to: Collaboration, Code Quality)

At Agricarbon, I mentored junior developers through 1:1 sessions and restructured epics with story points, reducing ticket count by 30–50%. This improved team velocity and code quality while fostering a collaborative learning environment.

---

## 5. Core Interview Questions & Rehearsed Answers

These are the most likely questions based on the job spec. Rehearse each answer to be 60–120 seconds when spoken aloud.

### Q: Tell us about yourself.

I'm Byron, a fullstack software engineer based in Cape Town with 3+ years of professional experience. I started at Mohara as an associate and progressed to tech lead, where I now own an enterprise product end-to-end — from architecture and client demos to production deployments. My core stack is TypeScript, React, Next.js, and Python/Django, and I've built a strong track record of performance optimisation, rapid bug resolution, and AI-augmented development. I'm also fluent in Afrikaans, which I know is close to LekkeSlaap's heart. I'm excited about this role because it combines high-traffic consumer products, a modern tech stack, and a team that values ownership and innovation.

### Q: Why LekkeSlaap / Tripco?

Three reasons. First, LekkeSlaap is a product I genuinely admire — it's a homegrown South African platform that competes with international players through superior local knowledge and user experience. Second, the engineering culture resonates with me: a close-knit team, cross-functional collaboration, and a clear emphasis on AI and automation as strategic priorities — that's exactly how I like to work. Third, I'm based in Cape Town and the V&A; Waterfront hybrid model is ideal. I want to contribute to a product that millions of South Africans use.

### Q: Your stack is TypeScript/React/Django. How would you handle our PHP/Laravel backend?

I actually see this as a strength, not a gap. At Agricarbon, I joined a Django/Python project having never used either professionally — I taught myself on the job while maintaining delivery on a fixed deadline. Laravel and Django share architectural DNA: MVC patterns, ORMs, migrations, middleware, and templating engines. I'm already comfortable with MySQL from my Agricarbon work. My approach would be the same: immerse myself, pair with experienced teammates, and get productive fast. I'm not married to any framework — I'm focused on solving problems well.

### Q: How do you use AI in your development workflow?

AI is deeply embedded in how I work. At Mohara, I didn't just use AI tools — I helped shape the company's AI standards and led knowledge-sharing sessions. Day-to-day, I use AI for code generation, debugging assistance, writing tests, improving documentation, and exploring architectural approaches. But I'm thoughtful about it: I review AI output critically, I understand the limitations, and I focus on using it to amplify my productivity rather than replace understanding. I'd be excited to help LekkeSlaap expand AI-driven automation in testing, deployments, and product features.

### Q: Describe a time you optimised performance on a production system.

On EA Inclusion, our main dashboard was loading in 48 seconds — a crisis because the client was scaling usage. I profiled the system and found the root cause was unoptimised database queries hitting PostgreSQL without proper indexing. I refactored the query architecture, added strategic indexes, and restructured how data was fetched. Load time dropped to 3.6 seconds — a 92% improvement. The key lesson was that performance is often about data access patterns, not just frontend optimisation, which I know is critical for a high-traffic platform like LekkeSlaap.

### Q: How do you approach owning a feature end-to-end?

I live this daily as the solo tech lead on EA Inclusion. My process is: understand the business need through stakeholder conversations, break it into technical tasks with clear acceptance criteria, implement across the full stack, write tests, deploy with feature flags for safe rollout, and then own the monitoring and iteration. I also run client demos and handle feedback loops. The feature flag system and structured RCA process I introduced have significantly reduced regression risk and improved release confidence.

---

## 6. Technical Deep-Dive Prep

Expect technical questions about architecture, performance, security, and their specific stack. Here are the key areas to prepare.

### Full-Stack Architecture (High-Traffic Travel Platform)

- **Caching strategies:** Redis for session management, page caching, search result caching. Discuss cache invalidation approaches.
- **Database optimisation:** Query optimisation, indexing strategies for MySQL/MariaDB, read replicas for scaling reads.
- **Search performance:** How would you build a fast accommodation search with filters (location, dates, price, amenities)?
- **API design:** RESTful patterns, pagination, rate limiting, versioning. How to handle booking transactions atomically.
- **Frontend performance:** Code splitting, lazy loading, image optimisation (critical for accommodation photos), SSR vs CSR trade-offs.

### Security (Booking Platform Context)

- PCI compliance considerations for payment processing
- CSRF protection in Laravel (built-in middleware) and React forms
- Input sanitisation and SQL injection prevention via ORM
- Rate limiting on booking and authentication endpoints
- POPIA compliance for South African user data

### Infrastructure & DevOps Talking Points

- Docker containerisation: multi-stage builds, docker-compose for local dev, production images
- GitHub Actions: CI/CD pipeline structure — lint, test, build, deploy stages
- HashiCorp Nomad vs Kubernetes: Nomad is simpler, single binary, supports non-container workloads
- Terraform: declarative infrastructure, state management, plan/apply workflow
- Monitoring and observability: logging, metrics, alerting for a booking platform

### Quick Laravel Concepts to Know

- **Eloquent ORM:** Active Record pattern (vs Django's Data Mapper). Models map directly to tables.
- **Blade Templating:** Laravel's template engine. Similar concept to Django templates. Twig is also used at Tripco.
- **Artisan CLI:** Like Django's manage.py. Migrations, seeding, custom commands.
- **Middleware:** Same concept as Django middleware. Authentication, CORS, CSRF handled here.
- **Laravel Mix / Vite:** Asset bundling. Compiles JS/CSS for production.
- **Queues:** Laravel has built-in queue system (similar to Celery). Redis as queue driver.

---

## 7. Behavioural & Culture-Fit Questions

LekkeSlaap's job spec lists specific behavioural competencies. Here's how to address each one.

### Q: Collaboration: How do you work across disciplines?

At Mohara I work across Product, Design, and Engineering daily. On EA Inclusion I run client demos, align priorities with the product manager, and translate business needs into technical tasks. On Agricarbon I introduced story points and restructured epics to improve cross-team alignment.

### Q: Problem-solving: Describe your approach to a complex challenge.

I balance analysis with execution. When the EA Inclusion dashboard hit 48s load times, I didn't overthink it — I profiled, identified the bottleneck (database queries), implemented the fix (indexing + query refactor), and validated the result (3.6s). Analysis to solution in days, not weeks.

### Q: Adaptability: How do you handle learning new technologies?

I thrive on it. I taught myself Python/Django on a live project with a fixed deadline. My approach: pair with experienced people, build something real immediately, and fill knowledge gaps as they appear. I'd take the same approach with Laravel.

### Q: Urgency & Ownership: Tell us about a time you had to deliver under pressure.

7 critical bugs, 5 days, before a major client release. I triaged by impact, fixed them systematically, communicated progress daily to the client, and achieved 100% scoring accuracy. Ownership means not just fixing the code but managing the relationship and expectations.

### Q: Continuous Learning: How do you stay current?

I'm deeply invested in AI-augmented development — I shaped my company's AI standards and regularly lead knowledge-sharing sessions. I follow industry trends, experiment with new tools, and actively bring innovations back to the team. My progression from associate to tech lead in ~2 years reflects this growth mindset.

---

## 8. Questions to Ask Them

Good questions show genuine interest and strategic thinking. Pick 3–4 from this list.

- What does the typical development cycle look like for a new feature — from idea to production?
- How is the engineering team structured? Are there squads, and how do they align with product areas?
- What's the current balance between new feature development and platform maintenance/tech debt?
- How are you currently using AI and automation in the development workflow, and where do you see the biggest opportunities?
- What does success look like for someone in this role after 6 months?
- Can you tell me more about the self-managed infrastructure? What drove the choice of Nomad over Kubernetes?
- How do you handle deployments and rollbacks on a live booking platform with high traffic?
- What's the team's approach to code reviews and knowledge sharing?
- How does the digital nomad month work in practice?

---

## 9. Loom Video Strategy (Afrikaans Opportunity)

The application asks for a Loom video (max 5 mins), ideally in Afrikaans. This is a huge differentiator — most candidates won't do it in Afrikaans. You should.

### Suggested Structure (4–5 minutes)

- **Opening (30s, in Afrikaans):** Greet them warmly. 'Hi, ek is Byron...' Introduce yourself naturally. Mention you're Cape Town-based and excited about the opportunity.
- **Experience overview (90s, Afrikaans):** Walk through your journey: bootcamp → Mohara → associate to tech lead. Highlight the key projects (EA Inclusion, Agricarbon) and your growth trajectory.
- **Why LekkeSlaap (60s, Afrikaans):** Genuine enthusiasm for the product, the bilingual positioning, the Cape Town hybrid setup, and the AI-forward engineering culture.
- **Technical fit (60s, can switch to English):** Briefly mention your React/TypeScript strength, MySQL experience, AI-augmented development leadership, and willingness to learn Laravel.
- **Close (30s, Afrikaans):** Thank them, reiterate excitement, mention you look forward to discussing further.

### Tips

- Be natural and conversational — they said 'just be yourself'
- Don't read from a script, but have bullet points nearby
- Good lighting, clean background, look into the camera
- The Afrikaans doesn't need to be perfect — using it at all sets you apart
- Show personality: your surf/ocean lifestyle and Cape Town connection is relatable

---

## 10. Interview Delivery Tips

### General

- Pause before answering — take a breath, collect your thoughts.
- Structure answers in 3 clear points: context, action, result.
- Keep responses 60–120 seconds unless asked for more detail.
- Use specific numbers: '92% improvement', '7 bugs in 5 days', '48s to 3.6s'.
- Show enthusiasm for their product — mention specific features you've noticed.

### Addressing the PHP/Laravel Gap

- Don't be defensive. Lead with your Django self-teaching story as proof of rapid adoption.
- Show you've done homework: mention Eloquent, Blade, artisan, and architectural similarities.
- Emphasise framework-agnostic thinking: 'I solve problems, frameworks are tools.'
- If they ask a Laravel-specific question you don't know, say so and explain how you'd find the answer.

### Your Differentiators to Emphasise

- **Afrikaans fluency** — rare in engineering, directly valuable for a bilingual platform
- **AI leadership** — you don't just use AI, you set company standards for it
- **Solo tech lead experience** — proven ownership and accountability at enterprise level
- **Performance optimisation track record** — 92% dashboard improvement, directly relevant to high-traffic platform
- **Cape Town based** — no relocation needed, can commute to V&A; Waterfront
- **Rapid learning velocity** — associate to tech lead in ~2 years, self-taught Django

---

Good luck, Byron. You're a strong fit for this role. Your ownership mindset, performance track record, AI leadership, and Afrikaans fluency make you a standout candidate. Go get it.

1→# Open Questions - Awaiting Byron's Input
     2→
     3→These are unresolved decisions and ambiguities from the README and task requirements. Each needs a decision before (or during) implementation. Where I have a recommendation, I've noted it.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Architecture
     8→
     9→### ~~Q1. Are you comfortable using Inertia.js?~~ RESOLVED
    10→
    11→**Answer:** Yes. Using Breeze + Inertia (React) with API routes alongside for booking submission and status polling. See key-decisions.md #2.
    12→

    14→
    15→## Booking Logic
    16→
    17→### ~~Q2. Can a user book multiple room types in one booking?~~ RESOLVED
    18→
    19→**Answer:** Yes, multi-room bookings (Option A). See key-decisions.md #19.
    20→
    21→---
    22→
    23→### ~~Q3. How should guest allocation work?~~ RESOLVED
    24→
    25→**Answer:** Per-room-type guest count (Option A). See key-decisions.md #20.
    26→
    27→---
    28→
    29→### ~~Q4. Pricing model confirmation~~ RESOLVED
    30→
    31→**Answer:** Pricing model confirmed as correct. Prices ex-VAT, displayed VAT-inclusive with tooltip. See key-decisions.md #21.
    32→

    36→
    37→**Answer:** Custom schema with `customer` naming (not `guest`/`booker`), includes `special_requests` field, no phone number. See key-decisions.md #22.

    41→## Date Handling
    42→
    43→### ~~Q6. Date constraints~~ RESOLVED
    44→
    45→**Answer:** Min 1 night, no max cap, allow same-day check-in, block past dates (starting from today). See key-decisions.md #23.

file.content:
# Open Questions - Awaiting Byron's Input

These are unresolved decisions and ambiguities from the README and task requirements. Each needs a decision before (or during) implementation. Where I have a recommendation, I've noted it.

---

## Architecture

### ~~Q1. Are you comfortable using Inertia.js?~~ RESOLVED

**Answer:** Yes. Using Breeze + Inertia (React) with API routes alongside for booking submission and status polling. See key-decisions.md #2.

---

## Booking Logic

### ~~Q2. Can a user book multiple room types in one booking?~~ RESOLVED

**Answer:** Yes, multi-room bookings (Option A). See key-decisions.md #19.

---

### ~~Q3. How should guest allocation work?~~ RESOLVED

**Answer:** Per-room-type guest count (Option A). See key-decisions.md #20.

---

### ~~Q4. Pricing model confirmation~~ RESOLVED

**Answer:** Pricing model confirmed as correct. Prices ex-VAT, displayed VAT-inclusive with tooltip. See key-decisions.md #21.

---

### ~~Q5. What data does ChannelManagerService::book() expect?~~ RESOLVED

**Answer:** Custom schema with `customer` naming (not `guest`/`booker`), includes `special_requests` field, no phone number. See key-decisions.md #22.

---

## Date Handling

### ~~Q6. Date constraints~~ RESOLVED

**Answer:** Min 1 night, no max cap, allow same-day check-in, block past dates (starting from today). See key-decisions.md #23.

---

## User Data

### ~~Q7. What fields do we collect at submission?~~ RESOLVED

**Answer:** Auth user data (name, email) + optional special requests textarea on summary step. No phone number. See key-decisions.md #24.

---

## Currency & Formatting

### ~~Q8. ZAR formatting convention~~ RESOLVED

**Answer:** Match LekkeSlaap's format: `R 1 200` for whole numbers, `R 1 200.00` when showing cents. Use `Intl.NumberFormat('en-ZA', { style: 'currency', currency: 'ZAR' })`. See key-decisions.md #25.

---

## UX Edge Cases

### ~~Q9. What happens to persisted state after a successful booking?~~ RESOLVED

**Answer:** Option C. Clear state on successful submission, redirect to confirmation page. See key-decisions.md #25.

---

### ~~Q10. Booking history - show a "My Bookings" list?~~ RESOLVED

**Answer:** Option A. Just the status page for the booking just made (MVP). See key-decisions.md #26.

---

### ~~Q11. UI design approach~~ RESOLVED

**Answer:** shadcn components with LekkeSlaap brand colors, property images from JSON, and layout informed by their site patterns - but all within shadcn's design system, no custom CSS. See key-decisions.md #27.

---

### ~~Q12. Polling for booking status - confirmed?~~ RESOLVED

**Answer:** Yes, polling confirmed. Frontend polls `GET /api/bookings/{id}/status` every 2-3s. The ChannelManagerService returns data to the queued job (not the browser), which writes to DB, which the frontend reads via polling. See key-decisions.md #10.

---

### ~~Q13. AI showcase - which level?~~ RESOLVED

**Answer:** Minimum. CLAUDE.md (done), session transcripts via /export, Co-Authored-By on commits (automatic), README section on AI workflow, key-decisions.md (done). See key-decisions.md #16.

---

## Summary of Questions

| # | Question | Status | Answer |
|---|----------|--------|--------|
| Q1 | Inertia.js - yes or no? | RESOLVED | Yes, Breeze + Inertia hybrid |
| Q2 | Multi-room bookings? | RESOLVED | Yes (Option A) |
| Q3 | Guest allocation model? | RESOLVED | Per-room-type (Option A) |
| Q4 | Pricing model correct? | RESOLVED | Confirmed correct |
| Q5 | ChannelManager data schema OK? | RESOLVED | Custom schema, customer naming, special_requests |
| Q6 | Date constraints OK? | RESOLVED | Min 1 night, no max, same-day OK, block past |
| Q7 | Collect extra fields or just auth data? | RESOLVED | Auth data + special requests textarea |
| Q8 | ZAR format? | RESOLVED | R 1 200 format, Intl.NumberFormat('en-ZA') |
| Q9 | Clear state after booking? | RESOLVED | C - clear + confirmation page |
| Q10 | Booking history page? | RESOLVED | Just status page (MVP) |
| Q11 | UI design approach? | RESOLVED | shadcn + brand colors + property images + LekkeSlaap-informed |
| Q12 | Polling confirmed? | RESOLVED | Yes, poll every 2-3s |
| Q13 | AI showcase level? | RESOLVED | Minimum |

input.prompt:
You are creating the "Backend Implementation" section of an implementation plan for a LekkeSlaap OTA booking engine assessment. This is a Laravel Breeze + Inertia.js project with React.

Create a detailed, checkable implementation plan for ALL backend work. Include:

## Database & Models
- Bookings table migration (columns: id, user_id, property_id, check_in, check_out, nights, currency, rooms JSON, subtotal, tax_rate, tax_amount, total_price, special_requests, status enum [pending/processing/confirmed/failed], external_confirmation_id, attempts, created_at, updated_at)
- Booking model with fillable, casts, relationships

## Property Data & Caching
- PropertyService that reads property.json and caches it (Laravel file cache, 1hr TTL)
- Controller that serves property data to frontend via Inertia with Cache-Control headers
- Property data "rarely changes hour-to-hour" - optimize delivery

## Booking Submission
- StoreBookingRequest (Form Request) with full server-side validation:
  - check_in/check_out: required dates, check_in >= today, check_out > check_in
  - rooms: required array, each room has unit_id (exists in property.json), quantity (1 to available_count), guests (1 to max_guests * quantity)
  - special_requests: optional string, max 500 chars
- Server-side price recalculation (source of truth):
  - per_unit: price * nights * quantity
  - per_person: price * guests * nights * quantity
  - subtotal = sum of all rooms
  - tax = subtotal * 0.15
  - total = subtotal + tax
- Price mismatch rejection (if client-submitted total != server-calculated total)
- BookingController@store: validate, recalculate price, save to DB with status "pending", dispatch job, return booking ID

## Queue Job
- ProcessBookingJob: calls ChannelManagerService::book()
- Retry 3 times with exponential backoff
- On success: update booking status to "confirmed", save external_confirmation_id
- On failure (after all retries): update booking status to "failed"
- QUEUE_CONNECTION=database

## API Routes
- POST /bookings (Inertia, auth required) - submit booking
- GET /api/bookings/{id}/status (auth required) - polling endpoint, returns JSON {status, external_confirmation_id, updated_at}

## ChannelManagerService Integration
- Place the provided ChannelManagerService.php in app/Services/
- The book() method accepts array $bookingData - format per key-decisions.md #22 schema

## Inertia Routes
- GET / or GET /booking - main booking page (Inertia page, serves property data)

Output each item as a numbered, checkable task. Group by the sections above. Include file paths where each piece of code should go. Be specific about validation rules and data types.
input.name: backend-planner

input.prompt:
You are creating the "Frontend Implementation" section of an implementation plan for a LekkeSlaap OTA booking engine assessment. This is a React + TypeScript + Inertia.js project with shadcn/ui, Zustand, React Hook Form, and Zod.

Create a detailed, checkable implementation plan for ALL frontend work. Include:

## Zustand Store (resources/js/Stores/useBookingStore.ts)
- Wizard state: currentStep (1-5), dates (checkIn, checkOut), rooms (array of {unitId, quantity, guests}), specialRequests
- persist middleware with localStorage
- Actions: setDates, setRooms, setSpecialRequests, nextStep, prevStep, goToStep, reset
- State survives refresh and auth redirect

## Zod Schemas (resources/js/Lib/schemas/)
- dateSchema: checkIn (date string, >= today), checkOut (date string, > checkIn)
- roomSchema: unitId (number), quantity (number, min 1), guests (number, min 1). Refine: guests <= maxGuests * quantity
- bookingSchema: combines date + rooms + specialRequests
- These are used by React Hook Form via zodResolver

## Shared Utils (resources/js/Lib/utils/)
- formatCurrency: ZAR formatting using Intl.NumberFormat('en-ZA', {style:'currency', currency:'ZAR'})
- calculatePrice: given rooms, nights, and property data, calculate subtotal, tax (15%), total
  - per_unit: price * nights * quantity
  - per_person: price * guests * nights * quantity
- cn: class merging utility (shadcn standard)

## Booking Wizard Component (resources/js/Components/Booking/BookingWizard.tsx)
- Orchestrates all steps
- Step indicator/progress bar (accessible, ARIA)
- Back/Next navigation
- Reads from Zustand store
- Each step validates via React Hook Form before allowing next

## Step 1: Date Selection (DateStep.tsx)
- Date picker for check-in and check-out (shadcn Calendar/DatePicker)
- Validation: check-in >= today, check-out > check-in
- Display number of nights calculated
- Block past dates in the picker

## Step 2: Room & Guest Selection (RoomStep.tsx)
- Display room cards from property data (name, description, price, max_guests, available_count, image)
- For each room type: quantity selector (0 to available_count), guest count input (up to max_guests * quantity)
- Real-time price calculation displayed per room and as running total
- Property images from property.json displayed in cards
- per_unit rooms: show "R X per night"
- per_person rooms: show "R X per person per night"

## Step 3: Booking Summary (SummaryStep.tsx)
- Display all selections: dates, nights, rooms, guest counts
- Full price breakdown per room type
- Subtotal, VAT (15% tooltip over total using shadcn Tooltip), Grand Total
- Special requests textarea (optional, max 500 chars)
- If NOT authenticated: show login/signup prompt (link to Breeze auth pages)
- If authenticated: show "Confirm Booking" button
- Auth user name and email displayed (auto-filled from Inertia auth props)

## Step 4: Submission (handled by SummaryStep confirm action)
- POST to /bookings via Inertia router
- Show loading spinner during submission
- On success: advance to status step

## Step 5: Booking Status (StatusStep.tsx)
- Poll GET /api/bookings/{id}/status every 2-3 seconds
- Show current status: pending (with spinner), confirmed (with confirmation ID), failed (with retry option or message)
- Stop polling when status is confirmed or failed
- Display booking summary alongside status
- Clear Zustand store on reaching this step

## Layout & Styling
- Brand colors: orange #EC772D (primary/CTAs), dark gray #5A5B5D (text), white (background)
- Property header with name and image from property.json
- Responsive layout
- Accessible: keyboard navigation, ARIA labels, focus management on step transitions, semantic HTML

## Auth Integration
- Inertia provides auth.user in page props
- Check auth status before showing submit button
- Login/signup links navigate to Breeze auth pages (state persists in localStorage via Zustand)

Output each item as a numbered, checkable task. Group by the sections above. Include file paths. Be specific about component props, state shape, and behavior.
input.name: frontend-planner

- Show loading spinner during submission
- On success: advance to status step

## Step 5: Booking Status (StatusStep.tsx)
- Poll GET /api/bookings/{id}/status every 2-3 seconds
- Show current status: pending (with spinner), confirmed (with confirmation ID), failed (with retry option or message)
- Stop polling when status is confirmed or failed
- Display booking summary alongside status
- Clear Zustand store on reaching this step

## Layout & Styling
- Brand colors: orange #EC772D (primary/CTAs), dark gray #5A5B5D (text), white (background)
- Property header with name and image from property.json
- Responsive layout
- Accessible: keyboard navigation, ARIA labels, focus management on step transitions, semantic HTML

## Auth Integration
- Inertia provides auth.user in page props
- Check auth status before showing submit button
- Login/signup links navigate to Breeze auth pages (state persists in localStorage via Zustand)

Output each item as a numbered, checkable task. Group by the sections above. Include file paths. Be specific about component props, state shape, and behavior.

You are creating the "Testing, Polish & Submission" section of an implementation plan for a LekkeSlaap OTA booking engine assessment. This uses Pest (PHP backend) and Vitest + React Testing Library (frontend).

Create a detailed, checkable implementation plan for:

## Backend Tests (Pest) - Priority 1
Located in tests/Feature/ and tests/Unit/

1. **Booking validation tests** (tests/Feature/BookingTest.php):
   - Rejects booking without authentication (401)
   - Rejects invalid dates (past check-in, check-out before check-in, missing dates)
   - Rejects invalid rooms (non-existent unit_id, quantity > available_count, guests > max_guests * quantity, zero quantity)
   - Rejects empty rooms array
   - Accepts valid booking and returns booking ID
   
2. **Price integrity tests** (tests/Feature/PriceValidationTest.php):
   - Server recalculates prices correctly for per_unit rooms
   - Server recalculates prices correctly for per_person rooms
   - Server rejects booking if client-submitted total doesn't match server calculation
   - VAT calculated correctly at 15%
   - Multi-room booking price calculation correct

3. **Job dispatch tests** (tests/Feature/BookingJobTest.php):
   - Job is dispatched when booking is created
   - Job updates booking status to "confirmed" on success
   - Job updates booking status to "failed" after max retries
   - Job retries on ChannelManagerService exception

4. **API endpoint tests** (tests/Feature/BookingStatusTest.php):
   - GET /api/bookings/{id}/status returns correct status
   - Returns 403 if booking doesn't belong to authenticated user
   - Returns 401 if not authenticated

## Frontend Tests (Vitest + RTL) - Priority 2
Located in resources/js/__tests__/

1. **Price calculation tests** (__tests__/utils/calculatePrice.test.ts):
   - per_unit calculation: price * nights * quantity
   - per_person calculation: price * guests * nights * quantity
   - Multi-room total calculation
   - VAT calculation (15%)
   - Edge cases: 1 night, 1 guest, max guests

2. **Currency formatting tests** (__tests__/utils/formatCurrency.test.ts):
   - Formats ZAR correctly (R 1 200.00)
   - Handles zero, small amounts, large amounts

3. **Zustand store tests** (__tests__/stores/useBookingStore.test.ts):
   - Sets and retrieves dates
   - Sets and retrieves room selections
   - Step navigation (next, prev, goTo)
   - Reset clears all state

4. **Form validation tests** (__tests__/schemas/bookingSchema.test.ts):
   - Date schema validates correctly
   - Room schema validates guest limits
   - Rejects invalid data

## Polish & Documentation
- README.md with:
  - Architecture decisions summary
  - Setup/run instructions (step-by-step)
  - What you'd add with more time (from key-decisions.md Future Enhancements)
  - AI workflow section with link to transcripts
  - Assumptions documented

- Verify refresh persistence: fill form, refresh page, data is still there
- Verify error handling: what happens if booking fails after all retries?
- Accessibility check: tab through entire form, screen reader basics

## Submission Checklist
- Git repo clean, meaningful commit history
- README complete
- AI session transcripts exported
- All tests passing
- App runs with `php artisan serve` + `npm run dev`
- `php artisan migrate:fresh --seed` works

---

## Prerequisites and Setup Checklist

### 1. Environment Prerequisites

- [ ] **1.1** Verify PHP 8.2+ is installed: `php -v`
- [ ] **1.2** Verify Composer is installed: `composer -V`
- [ ] **1.3** Verify Node.js 20+ is installed: `node -v`
- [ ] **1.4** Verify npm is installed: `npm -v`
- [ ] **1.5** Verify SQLite3 is available (ships with macOS): `php -m | grep sqlite` (should show `pdo_sqlite` and `sqlite3`)
- [ ] **1.6** Verify required PHP extensions are enabled: `php -m | grep -E 'mbstring|xml|curl|openssl|tokenizer|fileinfo|bcmath'` (Laravel requires these; most are enabled by default on macOS)

### 2. Account and Service Requirements

- [ ] **2.1** Confirm GitHub account is available for final submission (repository link required)
- [ ] **2.2** No external API keys, Redis, Pusher, or third-party service accounts are needed - the entire stack runs locally with SQLite and the file cache driver

### 3. Questions to Resolve Before Starting

- [ ] **3.1** All 13 open questions in `open-questions.md` are confirmed RESOLVED - no remaining ambiguities
- [ ] **3.2** All 28 key decisions in `key-decisions.md` are confirmed DECIDED - no pending items

### 4. Project Scaffolding

- [ ] **4.1** Create a new Laravel project with Breeze + React + Inertia + TypeScript inside the assessment directory:
  ```bash
  composer create-project laravel/laravel booking-engine
  cd booking-engine
  ```
- [ ] **4.2** Install Laravel Breeze with React + TypeScript + Inertia:
  ```bash
  composer require laravel/breeze --dev
  php artisan breeze:install react --typescript
  ```
- [ ] **4.3** Install npm dependencies and verify the dev build works:
  ```bash
  npm install
  npm run build
  ```
- [ ] **4.4** Verify SQLite database is preconfigured (Breeze defaults to SQLite; check `.env` for `DB_CONNECTION=sqlite`)
- [ ] **4.5** Run initial migrations to confirm the database works:
  ```bash
  php artisan migrate
  ```
- [ ] **4.6** Start both dev servers to verify the scaffolded app loads:
  ```bash
  php artisan serve &
  npm run dev
  ```
  Visit `http://localhost:8000` and confirm you see the Breeze welcome page with working login/register links
- [ ] **4.7** Copy the provided `ChannelManagerService.php` into `app/Services/ChannelManagerService.php` (create the `Services` directory)
- [ ] **4.8** Copy the provided `property.json` into an appropriate location (e.g., `storage/app/property.json` or `database/data/property.json`) - **[BYRON INPUT NEEDED]** Decide on the canonical location for property.json. Recommendation: `database/data/property.json` since it is static seed-like data, not user-uploaded content
- [ ] **4.9** Copy `CLAUDE.md`, `key-decisions.md`, and `open-questions.md` into the project root so they are tracked in git

### 5. Configuration - Environment

- [ ] **5.1** Set queue connection to database in `.env`:
  ```
  QUEUE_CONNECTION=database
  ```
- [ ] **5.2** Create the queue jobs and failed jobs migration tables:
  ```bash
  php artisan queue:table
  php artisan queue:failed-table
  php artisan migrate
  ```
  Note: On Laravel 11+, `queue:table` may already be included. Verify by checking if `jobs` and `failed_jobs` tables exist after migration.
- [ ] **5.3** Set the application name in `.env`:
  ```
  APP_NAME="Lekke Localhost Lodge"
  ```
- [ ] **5.4** Verify `SESSION_DRIVER=database` (Breeze default on Laravel 11+) so sessions work correctly with SQLite

### 6. Tool Setup - shadcn/ui

- [ ] **6.1** Initialize shadcn/ui for the project:
  ```bash
  npx shadcn@latest init
  ```
  When prompted, select: TypeScript, default style, the CSS file at `resources/css/app.css`, and confirm the Tailwind config path. Accept Radix-based aliases (e.g., `@/Components/ui`)
- [ ] **6.2** Install the shadcn/ui components that will be needed upfront (avoids mid-implementation interruptions):
  ```bash
  npx shadcn@latest add button card input label tooltip dialog form calendar popover textarea badge separator
  ```
  Note: `form` pulls in React Hook Form + Zod + @hookform/resolvers automatically

### 7. Tool Setup - Additional npm Dependencies

- [ ] **7.1** Install Zustand for state management:
  ```bash
  npm install zustand
  ```
- [ ] **7.2** Install React Hook Form, Zod, and the resolver (if not already installed by shadcn form component):
  ```bash
  npm install react-hook-form zod @hookform/resolvers
  ```
- [ ] **7.3** Install date-fns for date manipulation (used by shadcn Calendar/DatePicker):
  ```bash
  npm install date-fns
  ```
- [ ] **7.4** Install Vitest and React Testing Library for frontend testing:
  ```bash
  npm install -D vitest @testing-library/react @testing-library/jest-dom @testing-library/user-event jsdom
  ```
- [ ] **7.5** Add Vitest config. Create `vitest.config.ts` at the project root with path aliases matching `tsconfig.json` and `jsdom` as the test environment

### 8. Tool Setup - Commitlint and Husky

- [ ] **8.1** Install commitlint and the conventional commits config:
  ```bash
  npm install -D @commitlint/cli @commitlint/config-conventional
  ```
- [ ] **8.2** Create `commitlint.config.js` at the project root:
  ```js
  module.exports = { extends: ['@commitlint/config-conventional'] };
  ```
- [ ] **8.3** Install and initialize Husky:
  ```bash
  npm install -D husky
  npx husky init
  ```
- [ ] **8.4** Add the commit-msg hook for commitlint:
  ```bash
  echo 'npx --no -- commitlint --edit "$1"' > .husky/commit-msg
  ```
- [ ] **8.5** Add a pre-push hook to reject lightweight tags (as specified in CLAUDE.md):
  ```bash
  echo 'git tag -l | while read tag; do if ! git cat-file -t "$tag" | grep -q "tag"; then echo "Error: Lightweight tag $tag found. Use annotated tags only." && exit 1; fi; done' > .husky/pre-push
  ```
- [ ] **8.6** Verify commitlint works by testing with a bad message:
  ```bash
  echo "bad message" | npx commitlint
  ```
  Should fail. Then test with a good message:
  ```bash
  echo "chore: test commitlint" | npx commitlint
  ```
  Should pass.

### 9. Configuration - Tailwind Brand Colors

- [ ] **9.1** Add LekkeSlaap brand colors to the Tailwind configuration. In `tailwind.config.js` (or `tailwind.config.ts`), extend the `theme.colors` with:
  ```js
  extend: {
    colors: {
      brand: {
        orange: '#EC772D',
        gray: '#5A5B5D',
      },
    },
  },
  ```
  This enables usage like `bg-brand-orange`, `text-brand-gray` throughout the app
- [ ] **9.2** Update the shadcn/ui CSS variables in `resources/css/app.css` to use the brand orange as the primary color (adjust the HSL values for `--primary` to match `#EC772D`, which is approximately `hsl(22, 83%, 55%)`)

### 10. Configuration - Laravel Backend

- [ ] **10.1** Install Pest for backend testing (if not already present with Breeze):
  ```bash
  composer require pestphp/pest --dev --with-all-dependencies
  composer require pestphp/pest-plugin-laravel --dev
  ```
- [ ] **10.2** Verify Pest works:
  ```bash
  php artisan test
  ```
  Should run Breeze's default tests and pass

### 11. Git Setup

- [ ] **11.1** Initialize the git repository (inside the `booking-engine` directory):
  ```bash
  git init
  ```
- [ ] **11.2** Review the `.gitignore` that Laravel generates and confirm it excludes `.env`, `vendor/`, `node_modules/`, and the SQLite database file
- [ ] **11.3** Make the initial commit with all scaffolded code:
  ```bash
  git add -A
  git commit -m "chore: scaffold Laravel Breeze + React + Inertia + TypeScript project

  Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>"
  ```
- [ ] **11.4** Create the main working branch following the naming convention:
  ```bash
  git checkout -b sc-assessment/feature/booking-engine
  ```
  **[BYRON INPUT NEEDED]** Confirm the branch name. The convention is `sc-{ticket}/feature/{description}`. If there is no ticket number, `sc-assessment` or just `feature/booking-engine` may be more appropriate.
- [ ] **11.5** Create a GitHub repository and set it as the remote:
  ```bash
  gh repo create lekkeslaap-booking-engine --private --source=. --push
  ```
  **[BYRON INPUT NEEDED]** Confirm the repository name and whether it should be private or public during development (it will need to be accessible to the assessors for submission).

### 12. Final Verification Checklist

- [ ] **12.1** `php artisan serve` starts without errors
- [ ] **12.2** `npm run dev` starts Vite without errors
- [ ] **12.3** Visiting `http://localhost:8000` shows the Breeze welcome page
- [ ] **12.4** Register and login flow works (Breeze auth)
- [ ] **12.5** `php artisan test` passes all default tests
- [ ] **12.6** `npx vitest --run` executes without configuration errors (no tests to run yet is fine)
- [ ] **12.7** `git log --oneline` shows the initial scaffold commit
- [ ] **12.8** Committing with a non-conventional message is rejected by commitlint/Husky
- [ ] **12.9** `QUEUE_CONNECTION=database` is confirmed in `.env`
- [ ] **12.10** Both `ChannelManagerService.php` and `property.json` are in their target locations and tracked by git

---

### Items Requiring Byron's Input

1. **Step 4.8** - Location for `property.json`: Recommendation is `database/data/property.json`. Confirm or choose an alternative (e.g., `storage/app/property.json`).
2. **Step 11.4** - Branch naming: Confirm whether `sc-assessment/feature/booking-engine` is the preferred branch name or if a simpler name like `feature/booking-engine` is more appropriate given there is no ticket system.
3. **Step 11.5** - GitHub repository name and visibility: Confirm the repo name and whether to start private (switching to public for submission) or public from the start.

### Time Estimate for Setup

This entire setup phase should take approximately **15-25 minutes** assuming all environment prerequisites are already installed. The bulk of the time goes to Composer/npm installs and verifying the scaffolded app runs correctly. This is well within budget for a 3-5 hour assessment.

## Backend Implementation Plan

### A. Database & Models

  - `check_out` - date, not nullable
  - `nights` - unsignedSmallInteger, not nullable
  - `currency` - string(3), not nullable, default `'ZAR'`
  - `rooms` - json, not nullable (stores array of room selections per key-decisions.md #22 schema)
  - `subtotal` - decimal(10, 2), not nullable
  - `tax_rate` - decimal(4, 4), not nullable, default `0.1500`
  - `tax_amount` - decimal(10, 2), not nullable
  - `total_price` - decimal(10, 2), not nullable
  - `special_requests` - text, nullable
  - `status` - string, not nullable, default `'pending'` (enum values: pending, processing, confirmed, failed)
  - `external_confirmation_id` - string, nullable
  - `attempts` - unsignedTinyInteger, not nullable, default `0`
  - `timestamps()` (created_at, updated_at)
  - Index on `user_id`
  - Index on `status`

- [ ] **A2.** Create Booking model at `app/Models/Booking.php` with:
  - `$fillable`: all columns except `id`, `created_at`, `updated_at`

  - `user()` relationship: `$this->belongsTo(User::class)`

  - `rooms.*.quantity`: `['required', 'integer', 'min:1']` + custom rule that validates quantity <= `available_count` for that unit
  - `rooms.*.guests`: `['required', 'integer', 'min:1']` + custom rule that validates guests <= `max_guests * quantity` for that unit
  - `special_requests`: `['nullable', 'string', 'max:500']`

  - Computes `$subtotal` as the sum of all room subtotals
  - Computes `$taxAmount = round($subtotal * 0.15, 2)` (using the property's tax_rate)

# Testing, Polish & Submission - Implementation Plan

This plan assumes the core application (Laravel Breeze + Inertia, React wizard, booking API, queue job) is already implemented. All tasks are ordered by priority and dependency.

---

## Phase 1: Backend Tests (Pest) - P1

These are the highest-priority tests because the assessment explicitly evaluates "system integrity and financial accuracy" and "no manipulated prices pass backend validation."

### 1.1 Test Infrastructure Setup

- [ ] **1.1.1** [P1] Verify `phpunit.xml` has a `<testsuite>` for both `tests/Feature` and `tests/Unit` (Breeze scaffolding should provide this)
- [ ] **1.1.2** [P1] Confirm `tests/Pest.php` exists and configures `Tests\TestCase` as the base class for feature tests (standard Breeze + Pest setup)
- [ ] **1.1.3** [P1] Confirm `.env.testing` uses `DB_CONNECTION=sqlite` with `:memory:` database so tests run in isolation
- [ ] **1.1.4** [P1] Run `php artisan test` once to confirm the Pest runner works with zero tests

### 1.2 Booking Validation Tests

**File:** `tests/Feature/BookingTest.php`

- [ ] **1.2.1** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking submission without authentication` - POST to the booking endpoint without auth, assert HTTP 401 (or redirect to login for Inertia routes). Use `$this->postJson('/api/bookings', $validPayload)->assertStatus(401)`
- [ ] **1.2.2** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with check_in date in the past` - Submit with `check_in` set to yesterday, assert HTTP 422 with validation error on `check_in` field
- [ ] **1.2.3** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with check_out before check_in` - Submit with `check_out` earlier than `check_in`, assert 422 with error on `check_out`
- [ ] **1.2.4** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with missing dates` - Submit without `check_in` and `check_out`, assert 422 with errors on both date fields
- [ ] **1.2.5** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with non-existent unit_id` - Include a room with `unit_id: 999`, assert 422 with error on `rooms.0.unit_id`
- [ ] **1.2.6** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with quantity exceeding available_count` - Request 6 Standard Rooms (available_count is 5), assert 422
- [ ] **1.2.7** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with guests exceeding max_guests times quantity` - For 2x Standard Room (max_guests=2), set guests to 5, assert 422
- [ ] **1.2.8** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with zero room quantity` - Submit a room selection with `quantity: 0`, assert 422
- [ ] **1.2.9** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with empty rooms array` - Submit `rooms: []`, assert 422
- [ ] **1.2.10** [P1] Test: `it accepts a valid booking and returns booking ID` - Submit a fully valid payload as authenticated user. Assert 201 (or 200), assert response contains `booking_id` (or `id`), assert booking exists in database with status `pending`. Use `$this->actingAs($user)->postJson(...)` and `$this->assertDatabaseHas('bookings', [...])`

**Implementation notes:**
- Create a helper method or `setUp()` that builds a valid booking payload based on property.json data (Standard Room, 2 nights, 2 guests, 1 quantity)
- Use `User::factory()->create()` for authenticated user
- Use Laravel's `RefreshDatabase` trait

### 1.3 Price Integrity Tests

**File:** `tests/Feature/PriceValidationTest.php`

- [ ] **1.3.1** [P1] Test: `it calculates correct price for per_unit rooms` - Submit 2x Standard Room (R1,200) for 3 nights. Assert server calculates subtotal as `1200 * 3 * 2 = 7200`. Verify in DB or response
- [ ] **1.3.2** [P1] Test: `it calculates correct price for per_person rooms` - Submit 1x Family Cottage (R650) for 2 nights with 4 guests. Assert subtotal as `650 * 4 * 2 * 1 = 5200`
- [ ] **1.3.3** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking if client total does not match server calculation` - Submit valid rooms but with a manipulated `total_price` (e.g., R1.00 instead of the correct amount). Assert 422 with error indicating price mismatch
- [ ] **1.3.4** [P1] Test: `it calculates VAT correctly at 15 percent` - Submit a booking, assert the stored `tax_amount` equals `subtotal * 0.15` and `total_price` equals `subtotal * 1.15`
- [ ] **1.3.5** [P1] Test: `it calculates multi-room booking price correctly` - Submit 2x Standard Room (per_unit, R1,200) + 1x Family Cottage (per_person, R650, 3 guests) for 2 nights. Assert subtotal = `(1200 * 2 * 2) + (650 * 3 * 2 * 1) = 4800 + 3900 = 8700`, VAT = `1305`, total = `10005`

**Implementation notes:**
- The price validation logic lives in the Form Request or controller. These tests POST full payloads and verify the server-side recalculation
- Use exact decimal comparisons (or `assertEquals` with delta for floats)

### 1.4 Job Dispatch Tests

**File:** `tests/Feature/BookingJobTest.php`

- [ ] **1.4.1** [P1] Test: `it dispatches a job when booking is created` - Use `Queue::fake()`, submit a valid booking, assert the job class was dispatched. `Queue::assertPushed(ProcessBookingJob::class)` (or whatever the job is named)
- [ ] **1.4.2** [P1] Test: `it updates booking status to confirmed on success` - Mock `ChannelManagerService::book()` to return success. Run the job synchronously. Assert booking status changed to `confirmed` and `external_confirmation_id` is stored
- [ ] **1.4.3** [P2] Test: `it updates booking status to failed after max retries` - Use `$this->expectException(Exception::class)` or test the `failed()` method on the job directly. After calling `$job->failed(new Exception(...))`, assert the booking status is `failed`
- [ ] **1.4.4** [P2] Test: `it retries on ChannelManagerService exception` - Assert the job class has `$tries` property set (e.g., 3) and `$backoff` configured. Mock the service to throw, dispatch the job, verify it throws (Laravel will retry)

**Implementation notes:**
- For 1.4.2, bind a mock of `ChannelManagerService` in the container: `$this->mock(ChannelManagerService::class)->shouldReceive('book')->once()->andReturn([...success response...])`
- For 1.4.3, instantiate the job and call its `failed()` method directly

### 1.5 Booking Status API Tests

**File:** `tests/Feature/BookingStatusTest.php`

- [ ] **1.5.1** [P1] Test: `it returns correct booking status` - Create a booking in DB with status `pending`, GET `/api/bookings/{id}/status` as the owning user, assert 200 and `status: "pending"`. Update to `confirmed`, GET again, assert `status: "confirmed"`
- [ ] **1.5.2** [P1] Test: `it returns 403 if booking belongs to another user` - Create booking owned by User A, authenticate as User B, GET the status endpoint, assert 403
- [ ] **1.5.3** [P1] Test: `it returns 401 if not authenticated` - GET `/api/bookings/{id}/status` without auth, assert 401

### 2.2 Price Calculation Utility Tests

Assumes a utility at `resources/js/lib/utils/calculatePrice.ts` (or similar) exporting functions like `calculateRoomPrice()`, `calculateBookingTotal()`, `calculateVat()`.

- [ ] **2.2.1** [P2] Test: `per_unit: calculates price as price * nights * quantity` - `calculateRoomPrice({ pricing_model: 'per_unit', price: 1200, nights: 3, quantity: 2, guests: 4 })` returns `7200`
- [ ] **2.2.2** [P2] Test: `per_person: calculates price as price * guests * nights * quantity` - `calculateRoomPrice({ pricing_model: 'per_person', price: 650, nights: 2, quantity: 1, guests: 4 })` returns `5200`
- [ ] **2.2.3** [P2] Test: `multi-room total sums all room subtotals` - Pass an array of two room selections, assert the total equals the sum of individual calculations
- [ ] **2.2.4** [P2] Test: `VAT calculation at 15 percent` - `calculateVat(10000)` returns `1500`. `calculateTotal(10000)` returns `11500`
- [ ] **2.2.5** [P2] Test: `edge case: 1 night, 1 guest, 1 quantity per_person` - `calculateRoomPrice({ pricing_model: 'per_person', price: 250, nights: 1, quantity: 1, guests: 1 })` returns `250`
- [ ] **2.2.6** [P3] Test: `edge case: max guests for per_unit (guests ignored in calculation)` - Verify that changing `guests` does NOT affect the result for `per_unit` rooms

### 2.3 Currency Formatting Tests

Assumes a utility at `resources/js/lib/utils/formatCurrency.ts`.

- [ ] **2.3.2** [P2] Test: `handles zero` - `formatCurrency(0)` outputs `R 0.00` or `R 0,00` (locale-dependent)
- [ ] **2.3.3** [P3] Test: `handles large amounts` - `formatCurrency(125000)` outputs string containing `125 000`
- [ ] **2.3.4** [P3] Test: `handles decimal amounts` - `formatCurrency(1200.50)` includes the cents portion

### 2.4 Zustand Store Tests

Assumes store at `resources/js/stores/useBookingStore.ts`.

- [ ] **2.4.2** [P2] Test: `sets and retrieves room selections` - Call `setRooms([{ unit_id: 1, quantity: 2, guests: 3 }])`, assert `getState().rooms` matches

# Testing, Polish & Submission - Implementation Plan

This plan assumes the core application (Laravel Breeze + Inertia, React wizard, booking API, queue job) is already implemented. All tasks are ordered by priority and dependency.

---

## Phase 1: Backend Tests (Pest) - P1

These are the highest-priority tests because the assessment explicitly evaluates "system integrity and financial accuracy" and "no manipulated prices pass backend validation."

### 1.1 Test Infrastructure Setup

- [ ] **1.1.1** [P1] Verify `phpunit.xml` has a `<testsuite>` for both `tests/Feature` and `tests/Unit` (Breeze scaffolding should provide this)
- [ ] **1.1.2** [P1] Confirm `tests/Pest.php` exists and configures `Tests\TestCase` as the base class for feature tests (standard Breeze + Pest setup)
- [ ] **1.1.3** [P1] Confirm `.env.testing` uses `DB_CONNECTION=sqlite` with `:memory:` database so tests run in isolation
- [ ] **1.1.4** [P1] Run `php artisan test` once to confirm the Pest runner works with zero tests

### 1.2 Booking Validation Tests

**File:** `tests/Feature/BookingTest.php`

- [ ] **1.2.1** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking submission without authentication` - POST to the booking endpoint without auth, assert HTTP 401 (or redirect to login for Inertia routes). Use `$this->postJson('/api/bookings', $validPayload)->assertStatus(401)`
- [ ] **1.2.2** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with check_in date in the past` - Submit with `check_in` set to yesterday, assert HTTP 422 with validation error on `check_in` field
- [ ] **1.2.3** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with check_out before check_in` - Submit with `check_out` earlier than `check_in`, assert 422 with error on `check_out`
- [ ] **1.2.4** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with missing dates` - Submit without `check_in` and `check_out`, assert 422 with errors on both date fields
- [ ] **1.2.5** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with non-existent unit_id` - Include a room with `unit_id: 999`, assert 422 with error on `rooms.0.unit_id`
- [ ] **1.2.6** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with quantity exceeding available_count` - Request 6 Standard Rooms (available_count is 5), assert 422
- [ ] **1.2.7** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with guests exceeding max_guests times quantity` - For 2x Standard Room (max_guests=2), set guests to 5, assert 422
- [ ] **1.2.8** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with zero room quantity` - Submit a room selection with `quantity: 0`, assert 422
- [ ] **1.2.9** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking with empty rooms array` - Submit `rooms: []`, assert 422
- [ ] **1.2.10** [P1] Test: `it accepts a valid booking and returns booking ID` - Submit a fully valid payload as authenticated user. Assert 201 (or 200), assert response contains `booking_id` (or `id`), assert booking exists in database with status `pending`. Use `$this->actingAs($user)->postJson(...)` and `$this->assertDatabaseHas('bookings', [...])`

**Implementation notes:**
- Create a helper method or `setUp()` that builds a valid booking payload based on property.json data (Standard Room, 2 nights, 2 guests, 1 quantity)
- Use `User::factory()->create()` for authenticated user
- Use Laravel's `RefreshDatabase` trait

### 1.3 Price Integrity Tests

**File:** `tests/Feature/PriceValidationTest.php`

- [ ] **1.3.1** [P1] Test: `it calculates correct price for per_unit rooms` - Submit 2x Standard Room (R1,200) for 3 nights. Assert server calculates subtotal as `1200 * 3 * 2 = 7200`. Verify in DB or response
- [ ] **1.3.2** [P1] Test: `it calculates correct price for per_person rooms` - Submit 1x Family Cottage (R650) for 2 nights with 4 guests. Assert subtotal as `650 * 4 * 2 * 1 = 5200`
- [ ] **1.3.3** [P1] Test: `it rejects booking if client total does not match server calculation` - Submit valid rooms but with a manipulated `total_price` (e.g., R1.00 instead of the correct amount). Assert 422 with error indicating price mismatch
- [ ] **1.3.4** [P1] Test: `it calculates VAT correctly at 15 percent` - Submit a booking, assert the stored `tax_amount` equals `subtotal * 0.15` and `total_price` equals `subtotal * 1.15`
- [ ] **1.3.5** [P1] Test: `it calculates multi-room booking price correctly` - Submit 2x Standard Room (per_unit, R1,200) + 1x Family Cottage (per_person, R650, 3 guests) for 2 nights. Assert subtotal = `(1200 * 2 * 2) + (650 * 3 * 2 * 1) = 4800 + 3900 = 8700`, VAT = `1305`, total = `10005`

**Implementation notes:**
- The price validation logic lives in the Form Request or controller. These tests POST full payloads and verify the server-side recalculation
- Use exact decimal comparisons (or `assertEquals` with delta for floats)

### 1.4 Job Dispatch Tests

**File:** `tests/Feature/BookingJobTest.php`

- [ ] **1.4.1** [P1] Test: `it dispatches a job when booking is created` - Use `Queue::fake()`, submit a valid booking, assert the job class was dispatched. `Queue::assertPushed(ProcessBookingJob::class)` (or whatever the job is named)
- [ ] **1.4.2** [P1] Test: `it updates booking status to confirmed on success` - Mock `ChannelManagerService::book()` to return success. Run the job synchronously. Assert booking status changed to `confirmed` and `external_confirmation_id` is stored
- [ ] **1.4.3** [P2] Test: `it updates booking status to failed after max retries` - Use `$this->expectException(Exception::class)` or test the `failed()` method on the job directly. After calling `$job->failed(new Exception(...))`, assert the booking status is `failed`
- [ ] **1.4.4** [P2] Test: `it retries on ChannelManagerService exception` - Assert the job class has `$tries` property set (e.g., 3) and `$backoff` configured. Mock the service to throw, dispatch the job, verify it throws (Laravel will retry)

**Implementation notes:**
- For 1.4.2, bind a mock of `ChannelManagerService` in the container: `$this->mock(ChannelManagerService::class)->shouldReceive('book')->once()->andReturn([...success response...])`
- For 1.4.3, instantiate the job and call its `failed()` method directly

### 1.5 Booking Status API Tests

**File:** `tests/Feature/BookingStatusTest.php`

- [ ] **1.5.1** [P1] Test: `it returns correct booking status` - Create a booking in DB with status `pending`, GET `/api/bookings/{id}/status` as the owning user, assert 200 and `status: "pending"`. Update to `confirmed`, GET again, assert `status: "confirmed"`
- [ ] **1.5.2** [P1] Test: `it returns 403 if booking belongs to another user` - Create booking owned by User A, authenticate as User B, GET the status endpoint, assert 403
- [ ] **1.5.3** [P1] Test: `it returns 401 if not authenticated` - GET `/api/bookings/{id}/status` without auth, assert 401
